source: Ashby App A (6th ed., 2025) + ASM Handbooks + Bath ICE v3.0

materials.name: Aluminium/SiC MMC (20 vol% SiC particulate)
materials.notes: Particulate-reinforced Al alloy (6061 matrix). Higher stiffness and lower CTE than Al alone.

materials.name: Aluminum 2024-T3
materials.notes: Cu-Mg alloy; T3 = solution treated + cold worked. High fatigue resistance.

materials.name: Aluminum 5052-H32
materials.notes: Mg alloy; excellent corrosion resistance. H32 = strain hardened + stabilized.

materials.name: Aluminum 6061-T6
materials.notes: Most widely used structural Al alloy. Mg-Si alloy; T6 = solution treated + peak aged.

materials.name: Aluminum 7075-T6
materials.notes: Zn-Mg-Cu alloy; highest-strength common Al alloy. Lower toughness than 2024.

materials.name: Aluminum A356-T6 (Al-Si casting alloy)
materials.notes: Al-7Si-0.35Mg. Most common structural Al casting alloy. T6 heat treated.

materials.name: Aluminium foam, closed-cell (Alporas type)
materials.notes: Density range 0.2–0.9 × solid Al. Gibson–Ashby scaling: E ∝ ρ², σ ∝ ρ^1.5. Unique on Ashby charts.

materials.name: Aluminium foam, open-cell (ERG Duocel type)
materials.notes: Reticulated open network. Very high specific surface area — suited for heat/mass transfer.

materials.name: Aramid/Kevlar composite (60% Vf, Kevlar 49/epoxy)
materials.notes: Fiber-direction tensile properties. Poor in compression (fibers buckle). Excellent specific strength.

materials.name: Balsa wood (Ochroma pyramidale, along grain)
applications: Sandwich core panels (wind turbine blades, boats)
materials.notes: Lightest structural wood. Extremely distinctive on Ashby charts — ultra-low density end of natural materials.

materials.name: Bamboo (Moso, along grain)
materials.notes: Along-grain (longitudinal) properties. Tensile strength rivals mild steel at 1/8 the density.

materials.name: Basalt fiber composite (55% Vf, UD/epoxy)
materials.notes: Lower cost than CFRP; better temperature resistance than GFRP. Sustainable — made from volcanic rock.

materials.name: Beryllium copper C17200 (AT)
materials.notes: Highest-strength Cu alloy. AT (age tempered) condition. Beryllium dust is toxic — safety precautions required.

materials.name: Bone, cortical (human femur)
materials.notes: Properties along the longitudinal axis. Wide range reflects age/anatomical variation.

materials.name: Boron carbide (B₄C)
materials.notes: 3rd hardest material after diamond and CBN. Lightest hard ceramic.

materials.name: Brass C260 (cartridge brass, 70Cu-30Zn)
materials.notes: Most common single-phase brass. Excellent cold workability and corrosion resistance.

materials.name: Bronze C932 (SAE 660 bearing bronze)
materials.notes: Cu-Sn-Pb bronze; low friction against steel. The standard bearing/bushing bronze.

materials.name: Carbon-carbon composite (C/C)
materials.notes: Retains strength to >2000°C in inert atmosphere. Oxidises above 400°C — needs coating for air use.

materials.name: CFRP quasi-isotropic laminate ([0/±45/90]ₛ)
materials.notes: Balanced lay-up — in-plane properties isotropic. Much lower modulus than UD but directionally uniform.

materials.name: CFRP, unidirectional (60% Vf, T300/epoxy)
materials.notes: Fiber-direction (0°) properties. T300 carbon fiber, 60% Vf. Transverse properties ~10× lower.

materials.name: Copper C110 (ETP, electrolytic tough pitch)
materials.notes: 99.9% pure Cu. Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of engineering metals.

materials.name: Cubic boron nitride (cBN, sintered)
materials.notes: 2nd hardest material after diamond. Chemically stable in ferrous alloys unlike diamond.

materials.name: EPDM rubber (ethylene propylene diene)
materials.notes: Excellent UV, ozone and weather resistance. Operating range −50 to +150°C.

materials.name: Flax/epoxy composite (50 vol% UD)
materials.notes: Sustainable alternative to GFRP. Lower embodied energy and CO₂ than glass or carbon fibre composites.

materials.name: Macor (machinable glass-ceramic, Corning)
materials.notes: Unique: can be machined with conventional metal-working tools. No post-firing needed.

materials.name: Gold (pure, 24 carat)
materials.notes: Excellent corrosion resistance; extreme electrical/thermal conductivity. Very high density and cost.

materials.name: Graphite, polycrystalline (isostatically pressed)
materials.notes: Unusual: lubricating, thermally conductive, electrically conductive. Weak in tension, strong in compression.

materials.name: Hastelloy C-276 (Ni-Mo-Cr alloy)
materials.notes: Outstanding resistance to pitting, crevice, and stress corrosion. Ni-Mo alloy, no sensitisation.

materials.name: Polyethylene, high density (HDPE)
materials.notes: Linear chain; stiffer and stronger than LDPE. Most widely recycled plastic (code 2).

materials.name: Hydroxyapatite (HA), dense sintered
materials.notes: Main mineral of bone. Bioactive, osteoconductive. Brittle — not for load-bearing alone.

materials.name: Inconel 625 (nickel superalloy)
materials.notes: Ni-Cr-Mo alloy. Excellent corrosion and oxidation resistance to 980°C. Solid-solution strengthened.

materials.name: Inconel 718 (nickel superalloy)
materials.notes: Ni-Fe-Cr alloy, precipitation hardened. Workhorse of high-temperature gas turbines.

materials.name: Liquid crystal polymer (LCP, Vectra type)
materials.notes: Self-reinforcing; very low warpage and moisture absorption. Near-metallic CTE in flow direction.

materials.name: Polyethylene, low density (LDPE)
materials.notes: Highly branched; flexible and tough at room temperature. Widely recycled (code 4).

materials.name: Magnesia (MgO, sintered)
materials.notes: High melting point, good thermal conductivity, excellent chemical stability in basic slags.

materials.name: Magnesium AZ31B (wrought)
materials.notes: Most common wrought Mg alloy. H24 condition (strain hardened + partially annealed).

materials.name: Magnesium AZ91D (die cast)
materials.notes: Most common Mg die-casting alloy. T4 treated. Very good castability.

materials.name: Maraging steel 300 (C300)
materials.notes: Ultra-high strength with exceptional toughness/strength ratio. Ni-Co-Mo-Ti strengthened.

materials.name: Molybdenum (pure wrought)
materials.notes: 2nd highest melting point of metals after W. Very high stiffness-to-density ratio.

materials.name: Polyamide 6 (Nylon 6, PA6)
materials.notes: Lower Tm (220°C) and modulus than PA66 but tougher and cheaper. High moisture sensitivity.

materials.name: Polybutylene terephthalate (PBT)
materials.notes: Fast-crystallising semi-crystalline polyester. Lower moisture absorption than PET.

materials.name: Polyetherimide (PEI / Ultem)
materials.notes: Amorphous; excellent flame retardancy (UL94 V-0) without additives. Tg ≈ 217°C.

materials.name: Plywood, structural (Douglas fir, 5-ply)
materials.notes: Cross-ply construction — nearly isotropic in-plane properties. Stiffer and more uniform than solid wood.

materials.name: Polyamide 6/6 (Nylon 66, PA66)
materials.notes: Higher melting point (255–265°C) and modulus than PA6. Moisture absorbs → properties change.

materials.name: Polyimide (PI, Kapton-type)
materials.notes: No melting point (decomposes ~500°C). Continuous service to ~260°C. Excellent dielectric.

materials.name: Polysulfone (PSU)
materials.notes: Amorphous high-performance thermoplastic. Tg ~185°C; autoclave sterilisable.

materials.name: Porcelain (fired, technical grade)
materials.notes: Kaolin + feldspar + quartz, fired ~1250°C. Dense (< 0.5% porosity). Good electrical insulation.

materials.name: Polyphenylene sulphide (PPS)
materials.notes: Semi-crystalline. Very low moisture absorption; inherently flame-retardant (LOI ~44%). Often glass-filled.

materials.name: Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)
materials.notes: Semi-fluorinated; good chemical resistance. β-phase is strongly piezoelectric and pyroelectric.

materials.name: Silicon nitride (Si₃N₄, RBSN/HPSN)
materials.notes: Best combination of strength and toughness of engineering ceramics. HPSN (hot-pressed) at high end.

materials.name: Silver (pure)
materials.notes: Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of all metals. Tarnishes in H₂S environments.

materials.name: Stainless steel 17-4 PH (H900)
materials.notes: Precipitation-hardened martensitic SS. H900 condition (aged at 900°F / 482°C) = peak strength.

materials.name: Stainless steel 304 (18-8)
materials.notes: Most common austenitic SS. 18% Cr, 8% Ni. Annealed condition.

materials.name: Stainless steel 316L
materials.notes: Mo-bearing austenitic SS; superior pitting/crevice corrosion resistance. Low-C grade prevents sensitization.

materials.name: Alloy steel 4140 (Q&T)
materials.notes: Cr-Mo low-alloy steel, quenched & tempered at 315°C. Good toughness-to-strength balance.

materials.name: Alloy steel 4340 (Q&T)
materials.notes: Ni-Cr-Mo steel, high hardenability. Among the highest-strength structural steels.

materials.name: Bearing steel 52100 (hardened)
materials.notes: 1% C, 1.5% Cr steel. The dominant bearing steel worldwide. Hardened 58–65 HRC.

materials.name: Tool steel H13 (hot work, hardened)
materials.notes: Cr-Mo-V hot-work tool steel. Excellent thermal fatigue resistance. Hardened ~48 HRC.

materials.name: Tool steel M2 (high speed, hardened)
materials.notes: W-Mo-Cr-V-Co high-speed steel. Maintains hardness to ~600°C. Hardened 62–65 HRC.

materials.name: Stellite 6 (Co-Cr-W hardfacing alloy)
materials.notes: Co-Cr-W alloy; exceptional wear, oxidation and corrosion resistance. Cast or weld-deposited.

materials.name: Syntactic foam (glass microsphere/epoxy)
materials.notes: Hollow glass microspheres in polymer matrix. Near-constant properties with depth (no gas pockets).

materials.name: Titanium Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)
materials.notes: Most widely used titanium alloy (~50% of all Ti production). Alpha-beta alloy. Annealed condition.

materials.name: Titanium carbide (TiC, sintered)
materials.notes: Hard, high-melting refractory carbide. Used in cermet cutting tools (TiC+Ni/Mo binder).

materials.name: Titanium Grade 2 (commercially pure)
materials.notes: Most common CP-Ti grade. Excellent corrosion resistance. Lower strength than Ti-6Al-4V.

materials.name: Titanium nitride (TiN, PVD coating / bulk)
materials.notes: Electrically conductive ceramic (metal-like). Hard, refractory, chemically stable.

materials.name: Thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU)
materials.notes: Bridges gap between rubber and plastic. Excellent abrasion resistance and flex fatigue life.

materials.name: UHMWPE (ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene)
materials.notes: MW > 3.5 million g/mol. Outstanding wear and impact resistance; low friction.

materials.name: Zinc die casting alloy Zamak 3
materials.notes: Most common die-casting alloy. Excellent fluidity; good dimensional accuracy.

materials.name: Zerodur (lithium aluminosilicate glass-ceramic)
materials.notes: Near-zero thermal expansion (CTE ≈ 0 ± 0.02 µm/K over 0–50°C). Made by Schott AG.

materials.name: Zirconia, Y-TZP (yttria-stabilized tetragonal)
materials.notes: Transformation-toughened zirconia — highest fracture toughness of oxide ceramics (8–12 MPa√m).
